---
title: "Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision"
chapter_id: "claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "spinoza"
  - "viea"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "aletheia"
  - "uat"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim, and four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `coherence_exchange` remains connector/source-note mapped."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no claim-extraction or contradiction-detection tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "Lean coverage is limited to finite claim-update and promotion-review records; claim extraction, contradiction detection, and substantive belief revision remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `spinoza`, `viea`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Source loading state | passage-reviewed local raw cache: `spinoza`, `viea`, `aletheia`, `uat`; connector/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Claim/belief-revision fixture validation and `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` finite-record proofs are implemented; extraction and contradiction tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats claims as ledger records, not as truth because they appear in prose. The repo validates claim and belief-revision fixtures and implements narrow finite-record Lean predicates for prior-evidence/history preservation and open-contradiction promotion blocking. It does not yet run a claim extractor, contradiction detector, or automated belief-revision engine.

## Problem

The architecture needs a durable epistemic layer for claims, evidence, contradictions, uncertainty, and revision.

The VCM chapters decide what context is admitted and adequate. Reasoning now needs to decide what the system believes, why it believes it, how strongly it can say it, and what happens when evidence changes. Spinoza supplies the core discipline: neural systems can propose claims, but claims need structured support states, provenance, contradiction links, defeaters, and downgrade paths before they become commitments.

In the book itself, Appendix C is the public claim ledger. The architecture should generalize that discipline: prose is an output view, not the place where belief state lives.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs.

A fluent answer can contain assumptions that were never extracted, contradictions that were never linked, and claims that keep their confident tone after evidence weakens. Belief inertia is the epistemic version of technical debt: a claim remains in circulation because no artifact forces it to be revised.

Aletheia and UAT both push against this failure by decomposing outputs into propositions, preserving unsupported or inferred states, and routing uncertainty to review. The claim ledger gives those states a durable home.

## Core Claim

[claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Spinoza supplies proposer/verifier separation, proposition graphs, enforced tiers, contradiction-triggered revision, dependency tracing, and downgrade discipline. VIEA supplies artifact and claim-ledger pressure: important claims should become durable artifacts with support states, evidence relationships, provenance, and downgrade policy. Aletheia and UAT supply claim-native artifacts, verification gates, bounded review, retrieval-bounded proposition states, and SME adjudication routes. Coherence Exchange remains connector/source-note mapped speculative governance vocabulary, not external corroboration.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this claim-ledger claim to all assigned source notes, and four local raw-cache mappings now carry passage-reviewed references. The mappings support durable claim records, evidence states, contradiction links, uncertainty, review routes, and non-overwriting revision history as an architectural discipline, not a working claim extractor or belief-revision engine.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Passage-reviewed: proof-carrying hypergraph, proposition nodes with provenance/tier/entrenchment/dependencies/validity/versions/audit, justification hyperedges, enforced claim tiers, proof/refute/unknown outcomes, contradiction-triggered revision, dependency and unsat-core tracing, retraction, invalidation, re-verification, revision logs, and first-class certified/citation/procedure support tiers. | Does not prove open-domain claim extraction, natural-language formalization, or a complete belief-revision engine here. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: durable artifact graph, claim and verification ledger, verified/speculative/unsupported/contradicted/experiment-required states, claim extraction into support states, support/challenge relationships, evidence hierarchy, model-consensus limitation, provenance fields, and downgrade on expired, challenged, failed, or contradicted support. | No deployed VIEA claim-ledger system, runtime evidence trace, or claim-extraction test exists in this repo. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Connector/source-note mapped: structured claim/evidence units, verification supply chains, contestability, and fork/exit/audit governance framing for epistemic objects. | Epistemic-liquidity and economic metaphors remain speculative synthesis. |
| `aletheia` | Passage-reviewed: verification gates, per-node verification requirements, verification tiers, claim extraction and tier assignment, verified commitments in memory, claim-native artifacts with claim IDs, dependencies, and evidence refs, bounded adversarial review, omission/stability checks, contradiction-triggered belief revision, quarantine, human review, and new-evidence requirements. | No local Aletheia implementation, live-oracle run, adversarial battery result, or proof artifact is present. |
| `uat` | Passage-reviewed: human-in-the-loop SME force multiplier, retrieval-source-bound claims, atomic proposition decomposition, verified/inferred/unsupported states, dossier-bounded checks, SME_REVIEW handling, adversarial logic/citation/omission review, stability and hard-cycle caps, compression that preserves verified proposition counts, final SME sign-off, correlated-hallucination risk, and decomposition-loss risk. | Does not prove tribunal thresholds, reviewer independence, autonomous truth-machine behavior, or novel truth discovery beyond the declared dossier. |

## Mechanism

After context adequacy decides what a packet can support, reasoning needs a durable place for claims, uncertainty, contradictions, and updates. Spinoza supplies the internal mechanics: proposition nodes carry provenance, tiers, dependencies, validity state, versions, audit hashes, and justifications; contradictions trigger bounded revision by dependency slicing, unsat-core use where available, entrenchment-scored retraction, invalidation, re-verification, and logs. VIEA turns the same discipline into an execution-system rule: important outputs become artifacts, claims receive support states, evidence can support or challenge them, and expired, failed, challenged, or contradicted support causes downgrade. Aletheia hardens the artifact boundary by requiring claim-native objects, evidence refs, verification tiers, adversarial review, and explicit contradiction handling. UAT supplies the human review path for claims that are inferred, unsupported, contested, or vulnerable to dossier error. Coherence Exchange contributes connector/source-note governance vocabulary without becoming external corroboration.

A Belief Revision Record is the ledger transition that keeps claims separable from prose and keeps every support-state change tied to evidence, contradiction handling, uncertainty, and history.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Generated or extracted claim"] --> B["Claim record"]
  B --> C["Evidence refs + support state"]
  C --> D["Contradiction / defeater scan"]
  D --> E{"Revision needed?"}
  E -- "no" --> F["Retain with state"]
  E -- "yes" --> G["Revise / downgrade / block"]
  G --> H["Revision history"]
  F --> H
  H --> I["Claim ledger"]
```

- Extract claims into structured records.
- Attach evidence tier, provenance, and uncertainty.
- Revise beliefs when contradictions or stronger evidence appear.

The ledger separates claim text, claim label, support state, evidence refs, uncertainty, contradictions, revision reason, and review status. A claim can remain useful while unsupported, speculative, contradicted, or blocked, but it must be labeled accordingly.

Belief revision is not just promotion. It includes downgrade, deprecation, split, merge, contradiction preservation, and residual creation. Negative evidence is an asset because it prevents the system from relearning the same false confidence.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Belief Revision Record.

- VCM supplies source context.
- Spinoza verifies claims.
- Evidence matrix records book-level claims.

Minimum fields:

- `revision_id`
- `claim_id`
- `prior_support_state`
- `new_support_state`
- `evidence_refs`
- `contradiction_refs`
- `defeaters`
- `revision_reason`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_status`
- `history_refs`

VCM supplies source context. Spinoza verifies or downgrades claims. UAT can review contested claims. Appendix C records book-level claim support while preserving revision history.

## Invariants

- Claims are separable from prose.
- Contradictions remain linked.
- Revision history is not overwritten.

The non-overwrite invariant is central. New evidence can change the current state, but it cannot erase the fact that a prior state existed, which evidence supported it, and why it changed.

## Failure modes

- Untracked assumptions.
- Belief inertia.
- Contradiction deletion.

Untracked assumptions should become claims or residuals. Belief inertia should trigger review when contradictions or stronger evidence arrive. Contradiction deletion is unacceptable: contradictions can be resolved, bounded, or refuted, but they should remain linked.

## Minimal implementation

A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example.

The smallest useful implementation is the existing `claim_record` schema plus a `belief_revision_record` schema and fixture. The fixture should demonstrate conservative support-state handling without claiming that an actual contested claim has been resolved. The accompanying Lean module is intentionally narrower than a belief engine: it models finite claim-update records and promotion reviews; a valid update preserves prior evidence and revision-history references; and an open contradiction blocks promotion until it is resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual.

Those fixtures demonstrate record shape and conservative support-state handling. The Lean predicates do not show that prose claims were extracted correctly, contradictions were detected, or a substantive belief was revised.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Claim and belief-revision fixture validation | Check that claim and belief-revision fixtures match their public schemas and preserve support state, evidence refs, defeaters, uncertainty, review status, and history refs. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Claim extraction test | Check that prose claims can be represented separately from prose with labels, support states, and provenance. | planned; not run |
| Contradiction detection test | Check that contradiction refs remain linked instead of being overwritten. | planned; not run |
| Claim update preservation predicate | Check that finite claim updates preserve prior evidence and revision-history references when those references exist. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger`; builds locally |
| Open-contradiction promotion block predicate | Check that an open contradiction blocks claim promotion until handled. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger`; builds locally |
| Belief revision engine test | Check that support-state updates over extracted claims preserve prior evidence and revision history. | planned; not run |

Fixture-shape validation and finite-record proof predicates are implemented. The planned tests require a claim extractor, contradiction fixtures, and a transition checker that can reject unsupported promotion over real claim records.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:claims.ledger.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A claim update preserves prior evidence and revision history. | implemented |
| `lean:claims.ledger.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A contradiction prevents promotion until resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented only over finite Lean claim-update and promotion-review records. `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` checks that valid updates carry prior evidence and history references forward, and that an open contradiction blocks promotion. The proof does not implement claim extraction, contradiction detection, or a substantive belief-revision engine.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `aletheia` | Aletheia Foundry | safe_general_intelligence_lineage | Safe general intelligence predecessor. Use ideas; avoid final branding if collision remains. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |

The crosswalk identifies sources for claim-ledger and belief-revision discipline. Four local raw-cache sources now have passage-reviewed mappings, while `coherence_exchange` remains connector/source-note mapped. This does not promote any chapter claim merely because the repo has claim-record schemas.

## Summary

Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision makes belief state durable. Claims are separated from prose, tied to evidence and uncertainty, linked to contradictions, and revised without overwriting history. The reviewed sources justify the ledger boundary; they do not yet show a working extractor, contradiction detector, or belief-revision engine in this repo.

The chapter's rule for the living book is also the rule for the stack: a claim can be written beautifully and still remain unsupported until the ledger says otherwise. The next chapter takes selected ledger claims and asks what kind of proof, citation, procedure, or justification artifact would be required before their tier can rise.
